3: The ḥērem (ban/devotion to destruction) commanded against Amalek is the most theologically contested institution in the OT and has generated extensive scholarly discussion. Ḥērem designates the total consecration of persons, livestock, and property to YHWH — which in practice meant their destruction, since they could not be used for ordinary purposes once devoted. The institution appears in the Mosaic law (Deuteronomy 7:2; 1 Samuel 20:16-18), in Joshua's campaigns (Joshua 6:17-21; 1 Samuel 8:26), and here. The closest ANE parallel is the Mesha Stele (the Moabite Stone, ca. 830 BCE), in which King Mesha of Moab records that the god Chemosh commanded him to destroy the Israelite city of Nebo, putting all its inhabitants under ḥrm (the Moabite cognate of Hebrew ḥērem). This suggests ḥērem was a recognizable ANE category of sacred warfare in which the deity, not the king, is credited as the war's instigator and the spoils belong entirely to the deity. The theological problem — YHWH commanding the destruction of an entire people — has been addressed variously: as historical particularity (addressed to a specific people at a specific time), as the text's realistic portrayal of ancient warfare ideology being subjected to prophetic critique (Saul's failure is precisely his selective application), and eschatologically (as a type of the final divine judgment).
11: YHWH's 'regret' (wāʾinnāḥem) over making Saul king is a key theological text on divine repentance: niḥamtî kî himlaktî ʾet šāʾûl lᵉmelek — 'I regret that I have made Saul king.' The verb nāḥam in the niphal means to be sorry, to relent, to change direction — it is the same verb used in Genesis 6:6 ('YHWH regretted that he had made man') and in Jonah 3:10 ('God relented of the disaster'). The theological tension is explicit in 1 Samuel 15:29: Samuel says 'the Glory of Israel will not lie or relent (yinnāḥēm), for he is not a man, that he should relent (lᵉhinnāḥēm)' — yet YHWH relented in v11. The tension is not resolved by harmonization but represents the OT's honest portrayal of divine pathos: YHWH is genuinely affected by human choices while remaining sovereignly consistent in his covenant purposes. This is the background for the NT's theology of divine impassibility-in-engagement.
22: Samuel's oracle — 'to obey is better than sacrifice' — is the most concentrated statement of prophetic theology over against cultic theology in the pre-exilic period. The prophet-over-cult principle first appears explicitly here, though it is implicit in the Eli narrative. The pattern Samuel establishes — the prophet as the one who speaks YHWH's word in judgment over the king and the cult — defines the entire subsequent prophetic tradition: Nathan confronts David (2 Sam 12), Elijah confronts Ahab (1 Kgs 18-21), Isaiah confronts Ahaz (Isa 7), Jeremiah confronts Zedekiah (Jer 38). The prophetic word takes priority over royal and priestly acts. In ANE context, this is remarkable: in Mesopotamian tradition, the king was often the representative of the deity in both military and cultic matters, and prophets served the court. Israel's prophetic institution developed a distinctively adversarial relationship to the monarchy that has no close parallel in other ANE literature.
35: Samuel's grief over Saul and YHWH's grief over Saul run in parallel: ûšᵉmûʾēl lōʾ yāsap lirʾôt ʾet šāʾûl ʿad yôm mōtô kî ʾitabbēl šᵉmûʾēl ʾel šāʾûl — 'Samuel did not see Saul again until the day of his death, for Samuel was grieving over Saul.' The grief of the prophet for the rejected king is theologically significant: Samuel does not celebrate Saul's rejection but mourns it. The same grief marks YHWH in v11 — divine rejection is not divine indifference. The narrative portrays the cost of covenant unfaithfulness not as cold divine machinery but as divine and prophetic pathos. This motif of divine grief over the rejected runs through the OT into the NT: Jesus weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41), knowing the destruction his people's rejection will bring.
1: Samuel's formal commission to Saul — 'YHWH sent me to anoint you king over his people Israel; now therefore listen to the words of YHWH' — restates the basis of Saul's authority (the prophetic anointing) as the ground of his obligation. The šemaʿ (listen/obey) verb that opens the commission sets up the šāmaʿ-theme of the chapter: hearing/obeying is the issue. The prophetic word frames the narrative: YHWH speaks through Samuel; the test is whether Saul will hear. The connection between anointing and obedience is constitutive of the Israelite monarchy.
2: The historical ground of the Amalek judgment: 'Thus says YHWH of hosts, I have noted what Amalek did to Israel in opposing them on the way when they came up out of Egypt.' The reference is to Exodus 17:8-16 (the Amalek attack on Israel at Rephidim) and Deuteronomy 25:17-19 (the command to blot out Amalek's memory). The formulation pāqadtî (I have taken note of, I will punish) is the vocabulary of divine accounting: YHWH remembers and will act. The delay between the Exodus-era offense and the Saul-era execution shows that divine patience does not mean divine forgetting.
4: Saul mustered the people at Telaim — a site in the Negev region. The muster count (200,000 infantry and 10,000 men of Judah) follows the ANE convention of large round numbers for military census; the differentiation of Judah from Israel foreshadows the coming division of the monarchy. Telaim may be Telem of Joshua 15:24 in southern Judah, positioning the assembled force near the Negev for a campaign against the Amalekites in that region.
5: Saul came to the city of Amalek and lay in wait in the valley. The military preparation — laying in wait (wayya'āroḇ) — uses the vocabulary of ambush tactics. The 'city of Amalek' is unidentified archaeologically, as Amalekites were semi-nomadic and their settlements were less permanent than Canaanite city-states. The campaign is in the Negev-Sinai region where Amalekite territory lay.
6: The Kenite warning: 'Go, depart; go down from among the Amalekites, lest I destroy you with them. For you showed kindness to all the people of Israel when they came up out of Egypt.' The Kenites (associated with Jethro/Hobab, Moses's father-in-law; Judges 1:16) are historically distinguished from Amalekites and given prior warning. The ḥesed (covenant kindness) done to Israel at the Exodus is the reason for the exemption — the divine accounting that preserved Amalek's debt also preserves the Kenites' credit.
7: Saul struck Amalek from Havilah to Shur, which is east of Egypt. The geographic spread of the campaign: Havilah appears in Genesis 25:18 as part of the area where Ishmael's descendants settled; Shur is the wilderness border region east of Egypt. The full sweep of the Amalekite territory from north Arabia to the Egyptian border is indicated, though only Saul's partial compliance will be narrated in what follows.
8: He took Agag the king of Amalek alive and devoted to destruction all the people with the edge of the sword. The capture of the Amalekite king Agag — rather than executing him under the ḥērem — is the first violation of the divine command. In ancient Near Eastern warfare, capturing the enemy king was a prize of war bringing honor and potentially ransom or trophy value; Saul's sparing of Agag likely reflects this ANE convention of royal treatment for royal prisoners. But the ḥērem command makes no exception for kings.
9: Saul and the people spared Agag and the best of the sheep and the oxen and the fattened calves and the lambs and all that was good, and would not utterly destroy them. The specific enumeration of what was spared — the best animals, the good things — reveals the selective nature of the disobedience: the ḥērem was applied to what was worthless but not to what had value. The māʾăsû (they refused/were unwilling) signals the intentional character of the failure to execute the ban.
10: The word of YHWH came to Samuel — the prophetic reception of the divine verdict before Samuel has any information from Saul. The narrative emphasizes that YHWH knew of the disobedience before Samuel did; the divine word comes to the prophet as a revelation, not a report. The immediate divine response to Saul's disobedience — before Saul even returns — shows the speed of the divine accounting.
12: Samuel rose early to meet Saul, and it was told to Samuel, 'Saul came to Carmel, and behold, he has set up a monument for himself and turned and passed on and gone down to Gilgal.' The yāḏ (hand/monument) Saul erects at Carmel before going to Gilgal is the first recorded instance of a royal victory monument in Israel — the kind of triumphal stele that Assyrian and Babylonian kings erected after victories. This detail characterizes Saul's orientation: his first act after the campaign is to commemorate himself, not offer thanks to YHWH.
13: Saul's greeting to Samuel — 'Blessed are you by YHWH; I have performed the commandment of YHWH' — is a false report. The formula bārûḵ ʾattāh lADONAI (blessed are you by/to YHWH) is a standard greeting of blessing; its use here to introduce a false claim about obedience highlights Saul's self-deception. He has rationalized his partial obedience as full obedience, and he greets the prophet with a pious formula while making a false claim about his faithfulness.
14: Samuel's rhetorical question — 'What then is this bleating of the sheep in my ears and the lowing of oxen that I hear?' — exposes the lie without accusation: the auditory evidence of Saul's disobedience is unmistakable. The device of the evidence speaking for itself (the animals' sounds) is a literary technique that allows Samuel to let the facts make the accusation before stating it explicitly. The question form puts Saul in the position of explaining what cannot be explained away.
15: Saul's first defense: 'They have brought them from the Amalekites, for the people spared the best of the sheep and of the oxen to sacrifice to YHWH your God, and the rest we have devoted to destruction.' The deflection onto 'the people' (hāʿām) is noted by the narrator with irony: in v9 both Saul and the people together spared them. The religious justification — sacrifice to YHWH — is the first instance of Saul using religious language to cover disobedience. The form 'YHWH your God' (rather than 'our God') is a subtle distancing from the God whose command he violated.
16: Samuel's command: 'Stop! I will tell you what YHWH said to me this night.' The rhetorical stop (harap, desist/be quiet) cuts off Saul's self-justification. Samuel's authority rests on direct prophetic revelation — 'what YHWH said to me this night' — which supersedes Saul's defensive rhetoric. The prophetic word received in the night is now to be delivered; the urgency and the timing ('this night') emphasize the immediacy of the divine response to Saul's failure.
17: Samuel's confrontation begins with the historical reminder: 'Though you are little in your own eyes, are you not the head of the tribes of Israel? YHWH anointed you king over Israel.' The 'little in your own eyes' recalls Saul's initial humility before his anointing (1 Samuel 9:21: 'Am I not a Benjaminite, from the least of the tribes of Israel?'); what was genuine humility has become false modesty that excuses disobedience. The anointing is invoked as the basis of responsibility — greater honor, greater accountability.
18: Samuel recites the divine commission: 'YHWH sent you on a mission and said, Go, devote to destruction the sinners, the Amalekites, and fight against them until they are consumed.' The word wᵉhaḥăramtāh (you shall devote to destruction) is the verbal form of ḥērem; the commission is explicit and total. The Amalekites are characterized as ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners) — their guilt before YHWH is the basis of the ḥērem, not Israelite territorial ambition.
19: Samuel's direct accusation: 'Why then did you not obey the voice of YHWH? Why did you pounce on the spoil and do what was evil in the sight of YHWH?' The accusation names both failures: (1) not obeying (šāmaʿ) the divine voice, and (2) rushing on the spoil (wāṭṭaʿaṭ ʾel-haššālāl). The verb ʿūṭ (rush/pounce) used for attacking the spoil suggests rapacious eagerness. The double question frames the disobedience as both failure of listening and active transgression.
20: Saul's second defense repeats and expands the first: 'I have obeyed the voice of YHWH. I have gone on the mission on which YHWH sent me. I have brought Agag the king of Amalek, and I have devoted the Amalekites to destruction.' The threefold 'I have' (ʾăšer šāmaʿtî... wāʾēlēḵ... wāʾābîʾ... wāʾaḥărēm) is Saul's formal counter-claim. He asserts technical compliance — he performed the main action — while ignoring the specific exceptions. This is legal self-justification rather than repentance.
21: 'But the people took of the spoil, sheep and oxen, the best of the things devoted to destruction, to sacrifice to YHWH your God at Gilgal.' The third deflection to 'the people' and the religious justification repeated (sacrifice to 'YHWH your God'). The religious framing of disobedience — using worship language to cover unauthorized action — is a pattern Samuel will address in v22. The specification of Gilgal as the sacrifice location is historically significant: Gilgal was the site of Saul's earlier partial disobedience (1 Samuel 13:8-14).
23: Samuel's pronouncement on divination and idolatry as equivalents to rebellion: 'For rebellion is as the sin of divination, and presumption is as iniquity and idolatry.' The Hebrew terms: merem kᵉḥaṭṭaʾt qesem (rebellion like the sin of divination) and wᵉhapaṣar kᵉʾāwen ûtᵉrāpîm (stubbornness like iniquity and idols/teraphim). The comparison of disobedience to the Deuteronomically prohibited practices of divination and idol-worship places Saul's sin in the gravest category: not ritual error but structural apostasy. Because you have rejected the word of YHWH, he has rejected you as king.
24: Saul's confession — 'I have sinned, for I have transgressed the commandment of YHWH and your words, because I feared the people and obeyed their voice' — acknowledges the sin but identifies 'fear of the people' as the cause. The yārēʾtî ʾet-hāʿām (I feared the people) places the decisive weight on human approval rather than divine command. This is Saul's self-diagnosis and the narrator's understanding: the king who feared the people more than YHWH failed the test of covenant leadership.
25: Saul's request: 'Please pardon my sin and return with me, that I may bow before YHWH.' The request for forgiveness (śā nāʾ ʾet-ḥaṭṭāʾtî, please lift/carry my sin) uses the standard vocabulary of forgiveness. But Samuel's response (v26) denies the request — not because forgiveness is impossible but because the consequences of Saul's rejection are already set: 'YHWH has rejected you from being king over Israel.' The forgiveness of the individual sin and the removal of the kingdom are distinct.
26: Samuel's refusal: 'I will not return with you. For you have rejected the word of YHWH, and YHWH has rejected you from being king over Israel.' The structural parallelism: Saul's rejection of YHWH's word (māʾastā) is met by YHWH's rejection of Saul (wayyimʾāsᵉḵā). The verb māʾas (reject/despise) appears in both directions; the legal principle of reciprocal rejection — those who reject YHWH are rejected by YHWH — is the theological hinge of the narrative.
27: Saul seized the hem of Samuel's robe and it tore. The robe-hem (kānāp̄) of a garment was associated with identity and authority in the ancient Near East; seizure of a garment hem was a gesture of entreaty. In Akkadian legal practice, grasping the hem of the creditor's robe was a gesture of supplication. The tearing of the hem as Saul grasps at Samuel is a sign-act that Samuel immediately interprets as a prophetic symbol (v28) of the tearing of the kingdom from Saul.
28: Samuel interprets the torn robe: 'YHWH has torn the kingdom of Israel from you this day and has given it to a neighbor of yours, who is better than you.' The torn garment as prophetic symbol of divided/transferred kingship has an exact parallel in 1 Kings 11:30-31, where Ahijah tears his garment into 12 pieces to symbolize the division of Solomon's kingdom. The 'neighbor of yours, who is better than you' (lᵉrēʿăḵā haṭṭôḇ mimmekā) points forward to David — though David is not named here, the next narrative introduces him as YHWH's chosen.
29: 'The Glory of Israel will not lie or have regret, for he is not a man, that he should have regret.' The divine epithet nēṣaḥ yiśrāʾēl (Everlasting of Israel / Glory of Israel) is rare and distinctive. The statement that God does not niḥam (relent/regret) here appears to contradict v11 where God 'regrets' making Saul king. The solution lies in the distinction: YHWH can grieve over human outcomes (v11) without reversing his judicial decisions (v29). The relenting of v11 is emotional; the not-relenting of v29 is about the finality of the royal rejection.
30: Saul's third request: 'I have sinned; yet honor me now before the elders of my people and before Israel, and return with me, that I may bow before YHWH your God.' The request to be honored publicly before the elders reveals Saul's persisting preoccupation with social standing. He acknowledges the sin but his primary concern is not restoration with YHWH but preservation of his public image. The 'honor me' (kabbᵉdēnî nāʾ) request and the 'YHWH your God' formula (still distancing) characterize the depth of Saul's problem.
31: Samuel turned back after Saul, and Saul bowed before YHWH. Samuel's return with Saul is a concession to the public context — he does not abandon Saul publicly — but it does not reverse the prophetic judgment of rejection. The bowing before YHWH is the last act of official worship by the rejected king; the remainder of the chapter will be Samuel's execution of the ḥērem judgment that Saul failed to complete.
32: Samuel said, 'Bring here to me Agag the king of Amalek.' Agag came to him cheerfully. The characterization of Agag's approach as 'cheerfully' (maʿăḏannōṯ — or alternatively 'haltingly/trembling'; the Hebrew is contested) is ironic. Agag apparently believes that having survived the battle and been spared by Saul, his life is secure. His next words confirm he expects mercy: 'Surely the bitterness of death is past.'
33: 'Surely the bitterness of death is past' — Agag's statement is either confidence (death has passed me by) or recognition that death is near ('the bitterness of death is at hand'). Samuel's response — 'As your sword has made women childless, so shall your mother be childless among women' — invokes the lex talionis principle: the childlessness Amalek caused to Israelite families is now visited on Agag's mother. Samuel hewed Agag to pieces before YHWH at Gilgal — the execution site is where Saul had offered the unauthorized sacrifice (1 Samuel 13).
34: Samuel went to Ramah and Saul went up to his house at Gibeah of Saul. The parting is final and geographical: Samuel to Ramah (his home), Saul to Gibeah (his capital). The separation is enacted spatially. Samuel and Saul will meet one more time (1 Samuel 19:24 — Saul at Naioth) and once posthumously via necromancy at Endor (1 Samuel 28). The separation of prophet and king marks the unraveling of the Saulide monarchy — the king without a prophet is the king without a word from YHWH.
